---
title: "Positive-Definite Matrices: The Good Matrices"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / input directions
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # first eigenvector / max-curvature axis
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # second eigenvector / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the article where everything about symmetric
> matrices finally pays off as a *judgment*: which symmetric matrices are the "good" ones.
> It assumes the **spectral theorem** of [GEOM-16] — that every real symmetric matrix
> $A = A^\top$ factors as $A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$ with $Q$ orthogonal and $\Lambda$ real
> diagonal — and the **quadratic form** of [GEOM-17] — the scalar $q(x) = x^\top A x$ and
> the fact that diagonalizing it with $x = Qz$ turns it into the clean sum of squares
> $\sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2$. It also leans lightly on the dot product and norm ([GEOM-04]),
> orthonormal coordinates ([GEOM-05]), the transpose and the Gram matrix $A^\top A$
> ([GEOM-10]), and rank ([GEOM-12]). The one question here: *what does it mean for a matrix
> to be positive-definite, why is that exactly "the quadratic form is a bowl with a unique
> bottom," and how do we actually check it?* It sets up [GEOM-19] (Cholesky and the matrix
> square root, which exist precisely **because** a matrix is positive-definite) and
> [GEOM-24]–[GEOM-25] (a positive-definite Hessian means a genuine minimum). It is the
> object behind a **valid Gaussian** (a covariance must be positive-definite, Probability /
> Inference), behind **whitening and GLS** (Regression R11), and behind the **convexity
> test** that all of Optimization runs on.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A symmetric matrix is geometrically a set of stretches along perpendicular axes. This
article asks the one question that decides whether such a matrix is *useful*: are all of
those stretches **positive**? A matrix $A$ is **positive-definite** when its quadratic
form $x^\top A x$ is strictly positive for every nonzero direction $x$ — equivalently
(we prove it) when **all of its eigenvalues are positive**. We derive that equivalence in
full from the spectral theorem, then show what it buys: the surface $x^\top A x$ is a
**bowl with a single lowest point**, the matrix is invertible with a positive-definite
inverse, and a "square root" of it exists. We prove that the Gram matrix $A^\top A$ is
always positive-*semi*definite and is positive-*definite* exactly when $A$ has full column
rank — which is why covariance matrices and the normal-equations matrix $X^\top X$ are the
canonical positive-definite objects in data science. We give three honest tests
(eigenvalues, leading principal minors, and "a Cholesky factor exists"), run them on real
matrices including one sitting right at the semidefinite boundary, and state plainly when
the property holds, when it barely holds, and when its failure is telling you the modeling
problem is broken. The single claim defended throughout: **positive-definiteness is the
precise algebraic name for "this matrix is a bowl," and a bowl is the one shape from which
optimization, statistics, and geometry all get a unique, stable answer.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A symmetric matrix $A = A^\top$ is **positive-definite** (PD) when
$$
x^\top A x > 0 \quad\text{for every } x \neq 0,
$$
and **positive-semidefinite** (PSD) when the inequality is merely $\ge 0$. By the spectral
theorem this is *exactly* the statement that **every eigenvalue of $A$ is positive** (PD)
or **nonnegative** (PSD) — because in the eigenbasis the quadratic form unpacks to
$x^\top A x = \sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2$, a sum of squares weighted by the eigenvalues. So
the abstract inequality and the concrete eigenvalues say the same thing, and they both say
one geometric thing: **the surface $x^\top A x$ is a bowl that opens upward in every
direction, with its single lowest point at the origin.** That is the entire content of
"positive-definite," and it is why these are the good matrices: a bowl has a unique bottom,
so problems built on a PD matrix — minimizing a quadratic loss, inverting a covariance,
factoring out correlation — all have one stable answer.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. We will earn the equivalence "PD $\Leftrightarrow$ all
eigenvalues positive" line by line, then read off its consequences — invertibility, a
positive-definite inverse, a square root — and prove the one fact that makes the whole
property show up everywhere in data science: $A^\top A$ is always PSD, and PD exactly when
$A$'s columns are independent. By the end, "is this matrix positive-definite?" should feel
like the same question as "is this surface a bowl?", and you should know three ways to
answer it and what each costs.

# Why we are here: the matrices that behave, and the ones that betray you {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what makes a symmetric matrix
trustworthy, and how do we test for it before we lean on it?** Every step below answers
some piece of that.

The spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]) told us symmetric matrices are the well-behaved ones:
real eigenvalues, perpendicular eigenvectors, a free transpose for an inverse. But
"well-behaved" is not the same as "useful for the job at hand." Three of the most
important objects in all of applied mathematics are symmetric, and in each case the entire
question of whether the surrounding machinery works reduces to a single property of the
matrix.

**The covariance matrix.** A covariance matrix $\Sigma$ summarizes how the features of a
dataset co-vary. To use it as the heart of a Gaussian model — to write down the bell-curve
density in many dimensions — you must form $\Sigma^{-1}$, and the exponent of that density
is the quadratic form $-\tfrac12 (x-\mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)$. If that quadratic form
were ever *negative*, the density would blow up instead of decay, and there would be no
valid probability distribution at all. A Gaussian exists if and only if its covariance is
positive-definite. (The probabilistic meaning is Probability's to own; here we need only
that the matrix must pass a definiteness test.)

**The normal-equations matrix.** Least-squares regression solves
$X^\top X\,\hat\beta = X^\top y$ ([REGR-05], the multi-predictor sequel to [REGR-01]). To
get a *unique* line you must invert $X^\top X$ — and whether that inverse exists, and
whether the solution is a genuine minimum rather than a saddle, is precisely the question
of whether $X^\top X$ is positive-definite. When it is only barely so, the regression is
unstable (multicollinearity, [REGR-07] / [GEOM-26]); when it fails outright, there is no
unique fit.

**The Hessian.** The matrix of second derivatives of a loss function is symmetric
([GEOM-24]). Whether a critical point of that loss is a minimum, a maximum, or a saddle is
decided entirely by the definiteness of the Hessian there: positive-definite means a true
local minimum, the thing every optimizer is hunting for. "Is this a minimum?" *is* "is the
Hessian positive-definite?"

In all three, the symmetric matrix is doing its job only when its quadratic form points the
right way — strictly up. The history of the idea runs through exactly these problems.
Gauss and Lagrange, working on quadratic forms in the early 1800s, needed to know when a
form $\sum a_{ij} x_i x_j$ stays positive (so a least-squares problem has a real minimum);
James Joseph Sylvester in the 1850s gave the determinant test that still bears his name;
and the word *definite* entered the language precisely to mark a form that keeps a *fixed*
sign rather than wandering between positive and negative. The vocabulary was invented to
name the matrices you can trust.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why do we even need a special name — aren't symmetric matrices already nice?"* Symmetry
guarantees *clean structure* (real eigenvalues, orthogonal axes), but says nothing about
*sign*. A symmetric matrix can stretch some axes the right way and *flip* others — the
quadratic-form surface is then a saddle, not a bowl, and a saddle has no minimum to find.
Positive-definiteness is the extra condition that all the stretches point the same way
(all eigenvalues positive), turning the saddle into a bowl. Symmetry makes the matrix
*understandable*; positive-definiteness makes it *useful*. Almost every place a symmetric
matrix appears in data science, you secretly need it to be positive-definite, and the
half-failures (PSD but not PD, or "nearly" not PD) are where the real subtleties live.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: a bowl, a saddle, and a trough {#sec-intuition}

Before any proof, fix the picture. A symmetric matrix $A$ produces a *surface* over the
plane (or over $\mathbb{R}^n$): the height above the point $x$ is the single number
$q(x) = x^\top A x$. [GEOM-17] established that this surface is always a (rotated, scaled)
**quadric** — and that its shape is governed entirely by the *signs* of the eigenvalues.
There are three shapes, and positive-definiteness is the name of the first.

- **All eigenvalues positive — a bowl (paraboloid).** Every direction curves upward; the
  surface has a single lowest point at the origin and rises away from it on all sides. This
  is positive-definite.
- **Eigenvalues of mixed sign — a saddle.** Some directions curve up, others down. There is
  a critical point at the origin, but it is a *saddle*: a minimum along one axis and a
  maximum along another. The matrix is **indefinite**.
- **All eigenvalues $\ge 0$ but at least one equal to zero — a trough (degenerate bowl).**
  The surface is flat along the zero-eigenvalue direction — a valley floor with no slope —
  so the "minimum" is an entire line (or subspace) of equally-low points, not a unique
  point. This is positive-*semi*definite but not definite, and it is the honest boundary
  case we will keep returning to.

@fig-three-surfaces draws all three. The whole article is a way of making the leap from
"the surface looks like a bowl" to "the matrix is positive-definite" exact and checkable.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-surfaces
#| fig-cap: 'The three faces of a symmetric quadratic form $q(x) = x^\top A x$, decided by the signs of the eigenvalues. **Left (PD):** both eigenvalues positive — a bowl with one lowest point at the origin. **Middle (indefinite):** eigenvalues of opposite sign — a saddle, a minimum along one axis and a maximum along the perpendicular one. **Right (PSD, not PD):** one eigenvalue zero — a trough that is flat along an entire direction, so its minimum is a whole line, not a point. Positive-definiteness is precisely the leftmost shape.'
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11.2, 3.5))
g = np.linspace(-2, 2, 60)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)

mats = [
    (np.array([[2.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.5]]), "PD: bowl\n(both $\\lambda>0$)"),
    (np.array([[1.5, 0.0], [0.0, -1.0]]), "indefinite: saddle\n($\\lambda$ mixed sign)"),
    (np.array([[1.0, -1.0], [-1.0, 1.0]]), "PSD not PD: trough\n(one $\\lambda=0$)"),
]
for i, (M, title) in enumerate(mats, 1):
    ax = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, i, projection="3d")
    Z = (M[0, 0] * GX**2 + (M[0, 1] + M[1, 0]) * GX * GY + M[1, 1] * GY**2)
    ax.plot_surface(GX, GY, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.9, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$", fontsize=9); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_zlabel("$q(x)$", fontsize=9)
    ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

Why should the *signs of the eigenvalues* control the shape? Because, as [GEOM-17] showed
and we recall in @sec-form-in-eigenbasis, when you look at the surface in the eigenbasis
the cross-terms vanish and it becomes $q = \lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2 + \cdots$ — a
clean sum of upward parabolas, one per axis, each scaled by its eigenvalue. An upward
parabola $\lambda z^2$ opens up when $\lambda > 0$, opens down when $\lambda < 0$, and goes
flat when $\lambda = 0$. The whole surface is just these one-dimensional parabolas stacked
along perpendicular axes, so its global shape is read straight off the signs. The rest of
the article turns this picture into theorems and tests.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We begin with the definition that is stated in terms of the quadratic form, recall the one
fact from [GEOM-17] that diagonalizes that form, and then prove the eigenvalue
characterization — the workhorse of everything after.

## The definition, and reading it carefully {#sec-definition}

::: {#def-pd}
**Definition (positive-definite and positive-semidefinite).** Let $A = A^\top$ be a real
symmetric $n\times n$ matrix. Then $A$ is

- **positive-definite (PD)** if $x^\top A x > 0$ for every nonzero vector $x \in
  \mathbb{R}^n$;
- **positive-semidefinite (PSD)** if $x^\top A x \ge 0$ for every $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

The mirror notions are **negative-definite** ($x^\top A x < 0$ for all $x\neq 0$) and
**negative-semidefinite** ($\le 0$); a symmetric matrix that is none of these — its form
takes both signs — is **indefinite**.
:::

Three points of care, because the definition is more subtle than it looks.

**We require $A$ symmetric.** For a general matrix the quadratic form $x^\top A x$ only ever
"sees" the symmetric part of $A$: writing any square $A$ as $A = S + K$ with $S =
\tfrac12(A + A^\top)$ symmetric and $K = \tfrac12(A - A^\top)$ anti-symmetric, the
anti-symmetric piece contributes nothing, because $x^\top K x = (x^\top K x)^\top = x^\top
K^\top x = -x^\top K x$ forces $x^\top K x = 0$. (The first equality holds because $x^\top
K x$ is a $1\times1$ matrix, equal to its own transpose; the transpose rule $(x^\top K
x)^\top = x^\top K^\top x$ is [GEOM-10]; and $K^\top = -K$ is what "anti-symmetric" means.)
So definiteness is genuinely a property of symmetric matrices, and we lose nothing by
assuming $A = A^\top$ from the start. This is the same "symmetrize without loss of
generality" move that opened [GEOM-17].

**The strict inequality must hold for *every* nonzero $x$ at once.** It is not enough for
$x^\top A x$ to be positive at the standard basis vectors, or on average. PD is a statement
about *all* directions simultaneously; a single bad direction (one $x$ with $x^\top A x \le
0$) disqualifies it. This is exactly why testing one entry, or even all the diagonal
entries, is not a test (we will see a matrix with all-positive diagonal that is still not
PD in @sec-ex-edge).

**The $x \neq 0$ exclusion is mandatory.** At $x = 0$ the form is $0^\top A 0 = 0$ for
*every* matrix, so the origin can never witness positivity; that is why the PD definition
quantifies over $x \neq 0$. The PSD definition allows $x^\top A x = 0$ at nonzero $x$ too —
that slack is the entire difference between "bowl" (PD) and "trough" (PSD), and it is where
the boundary cases live.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the diagonal of a positive-definite matrix always positive?"* Yes — and it is a free
one-line consequence of the definition, worth knowing as a quick *necessary* (not
sufficient) screen. Take $x = e_i$, the standard basis vector with a $1$ in slot $i$ and
zeros elsewhere. Then $e_i^\top A e_i = A_{ii}$, the $i$-th diagonal entry, and PD demands
this be $> 0$. So **every diagonal entry of a PD matrix is positive.** The converse fails
badly: positive diagonal does *not* imply PD (the bad direction may be a diagonal one, not
an axis). Use "any nonpositive diagonal entry $\Rightarrow$ not PD" as an instant
disqualifier, never as a confirmation.
:::

## The quadratic form in the eigenbasis (recalled from GEOM-17) {#sec-form-in-eigenbasis}

The single fact we import — and it is the hinge of the whole article — is how the spectral
theorem simplifies a quadratic form. We restate it and re-derive it in three lines so the
logic runs end to end here, even though its full treatment is [GEOM-17].

Let $A = A^\top$ be symmetric. By the spectral theorem ([GEOM-16]) it factors as
$$
A = Q\Lambda Q^\top, \qquad Q^\top Q = I, \qquad
\Lambda = \operatorname{diag}(\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n),
$$ {#eq-spectral}
with $Q$ orthogonal (columns an orthonormal eigenbasis $q_1,\dots,q_n$) and the
$\lambda_k$ the real eigenvalues. Now feed a vector $x$ into the quadratic form and
substitute @eq-spectral:
$$
x^\top A x = x^\top Q\Lambda Q^\top x = (Q^\top x)^\top \Lambda (Q^\top x),
$$
where the last step just regrouped, using $(Q^\top x)^\top = x^\top Q$ (the transpose rule
$[GEOM-10]$). Define the **rotated coordinates**
$$
z = Q^\top x,
$$ {#eq-rotated-coords}
the coordinates of $x$ in the eigenbasis (each $z_k = q_k^\top x$ is a dot product, the
orthonormal-coordinates fact of [GEOM-05]). Then $x^\top A x = z^\top \Lambda z$, and because
$\Lambda$ is diagonal this last expression is just a weighted sum of squares:
$$
z^\top \Lambda z
= \begin{bmatrix} z_1 & \cdots & z_n \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_1 z_1 \\ \vdots \\ \lambda_n z_n \end{bmatrix}
= \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lambda_k z_k^2 .
$$ {#eq-diag-form}

This is the result we will use over and over:

$$
\boxed{\; x^\top A x \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \lambda_k z_k^2, \qquad z = Q^\top x. \;}
$$ {#eq-form-eigenbasis}

In words: **in the eigenbasis, a quadratic form is a pure sum of squares, each weighted by
an eigenvalue.** No cross-terms, no interaction — the eigenbasis is exactly the coordinate
system in which the form decouples. Two facts about the change of coordinates $z = Q^\top
x$ that we will need: it is a *bijection* (because $Q$ is invertible, with $x = Qz$), so $x$
ranges over all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ exactly as $z$ does; and it is *length-preserving*, since
$\|z\|^2 = z^\top z = (Q^\top x)^\top(Q^\top x) = x^\top Q Q^\top x = x^\top x = \|x\|^2$
(using $QQ^\top = I$). In particular $x \neq 0 \iff z \neq 0$. We use this equivalence
immediately.

## The eigenvalue characterization {#sec-eigenvalue-char}

Here is the theorem that converts the abstract, all-directions inequality of @def-pd into a
finite, checkable list of $n$ numbers.

::: {#thm-pd-eig}
**Theorem (PD $\Leftrightarrow$ positive eigenvalues).** Let $A = A^\top$ be real
symmetric with eigenvalues $\lambda_1,\dots,\lambda_n$ (all real, by the spectral theorem).
Then

1. $A$ is **positive-definite** $\iff$ every $\lambda_k > 0$;
2. $A$ is **positive-semidefinite** $\iff$ every $\lambda_k \ge 0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove part 1 in full; part 2 is the identical argument with $>$ relaxed to $\ge$, noted
at the end. Throughout we use @eq-form-eigenbasis: for every $x$, with $z = Q^\top x$,
$$
x^\top A x = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\lambda_k z_k^2 ,
$$
and the bijection $x \leftrightarrow z$ with $x\neq 0 \iff z\neq 0$ from
@sec-form-in-eigenbasis.

**($\Leftarrow$) Suppose every $\lambda_k > 0$; we show $A$ is PD.** Take any $x \neq 0$.
Then $z = Q^\top x \neq 0$, so at least one coordinate $z_j \neq 0$, giving $z_j^2 > 0$.
Each term $\lambda_k z_k^2$ in $\sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2$ is a positive eigenvalue times a
nonnegative square, hence $\ge 0$; and the $j$-th term is $\lambda_j z_j^2 > 0$ strictly
(positive times positive). A sum of nonnegative terms that includes a strictly positive
one is strictly positive:
$$
x^\top A x = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\lambda_k z_k^2 \;\ge\; \lambda_j z_j^2 \;>\; 0 .
$$
Since this holds for every $x \neq 0$, $A$ is positive-definite by @def-pd.

**($\Rightarrow$) Suppose $A$ is PD; we show every $\lambda_k > 0$.** This is the half where
we *test the form in a cleverly chosen direction*. Fix any eigenvalue $\lambda_j$ and feed
its own eigenvector $q_j$ (a column of $Q$) into the form. The rotated coordinates of $q_j$
are $z = Q^\top q_j$, and because the columns of $Q$ are orthonormal,
$$
z_k = q_k^\top q_j = \begin{cases} 1 & k = j \\ 0 & k \neq j \end{cases},
$$
i.e. $z = e_j$, the $j$-th standard basis vector. Plugging into @eq-form-eigenbasis, only
the $k=j$ term of the sum survives:
$$
q_j^\top A q_j = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\lambda_k (z_k)^2 = \lambda_j \cdot 1^2 = \lambda_j .
$$
But $q_j \neq 0$ (it is a unit vector), so PD demands $q_j^\top A q_j > 0$, i.e.
$$
\lambda_j = q_j^\top A q_j > 0 .
$$
Since $j$ was arbitrary, *every* eigenvalue is positive. This completes part 1.

**Part 2 (PSD).** Replace "$>0$" by "$\ge 0$" throughout. The ($\Leftarrow$) direction now
gives $x^\top A x = \sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2 \ge 0$ as a sum of nonnegative terms (each
$\lambda_k \ge 0$). The ($\Rightarrow$) direction is identical: $q_j^\top A q_j =
\lambda_j$, and PSD forces this to be $\ge 0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The ($\Rightarrow$) half is worth pausing on, because it contains the recurring trick of
this whole subject: **to learn about a quadratic form, probe it with the eigenvectors.**
Feeding $q_j$ into $x^\top A x$ isolates a single eigenvalue, $q_j^\top A q_j = \lambda_j$.
That identity — *the quadratic form evaluated on a unit eigenvector returns its
eigenvalue* — is the bridge between the geometric definition and the algebraic test, and it
is worth memorizing on its own.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me the fastest correct check that a symmetric matrix is positive-definite."* The
honest answer depends on what you have. With the matrix in hand and modest size, **compute
the eigenvalues and check they are all $> 0$** (@thm-pd-eig) — conceptually cleanest, and
exactly what the theorem licenses. In production code the faster and numerically preferred
test is **attempt a Cholesky factorization and see whether it succeeds** (@sec-cholesky-test),
because Cholesky exists *if and only if* the matrix is PD and costs about half a general
factorization. The classroom favorite is **Sylvester's criterion** — all leading principal
minors positive (@sec-sylvester) — which is what you use when checking a small symbolic
matrix by hand. Three tests, same property; knowing which to deploy when is the
practitioner's judgment.
:::

## Three immediate payoffs: invertibility, a PD inverse, and a square root {#sec-payoffs}

The eigenvalue characterization makes several "good matrix" properties fall out
immediately. Each is a one- or two-line consequence, and each is a reason PD matrices are
the ones you want.

::: {#cor-invertible}
**Corollary (a PD matrix is invertible, with a PD inverse).** If $A$ is positive-definite
then $A$ is invertible, $A^{-1}$ exists, and $A^{-1}$ is itself positive-definite.
:::

::: {.proof}
A symmetric matrix is invertible exactly when no eigenvalue is zero — because $\det A =
\prod_k \lambda_k$ (the determinant is the product of the eigenvalues, [GEOM-14]), and a
product is nonzero iff every factor is nonzero. Since $A$ is PD, every $\lambda_k > 0$, so
none is zero and $\det A = \prod_k \lambda_k > 0 \neq 0$: $A$ is invertible.

For the inverse, use the spectral form @eq-spectral. We claim $A^{-1} = Q\Lambda^{-1}
Q^\top$, where $\Lambda^{-1} = \operatorname{diag}(1/\lambda_1,\dots,1/\lambda_n)$ (each
$\lambda_k \neq 0$, so the reciprocals exist). Verify by multiplying, using $Q^\top Q = I$:
$$
(Q\Lambda Q^\top)(Q\Lambda^{-1}Q^\top)
= Q\Lambda (Q^\top Q)\Lambda^{-1} Q^\top
= Q\Lambda\Lambda^{-1}Q^\top
= Q I Q^\top = QQ^\top = I .
$$
So $A^{-1} = Q\Lambda^{-1}Q^\top$ — and this is again a spectral decomposition, of a
symmetric matrix whose eigenvalues are the $1/\lambda_k$. Each $1/\lambda_k > 0$ because
each $\lambda_k > 0$, so by @thm-pd-eig the inverse is positive-definite. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the corollary that makes covariance-based methods work: a positive-definite
covariance can always be inverted (to form the *precision matrix* $\Sigma^{-1}$ that drives
the Gaussian density and the Mahalanobis distance), and the inverse is again a genuine
covariance-shaped object — symmetric and positive-definite.

::: {#cor-sqrt}
**Corollary (a PD matrix has a positive-definite square root).** If $A$ is
positive-definite then there is a unique positive-definite symmetric matrix $A^{1/2}$ with
$A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Define $A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top$ where $\Lambda^{1/2} =
\operatorname{diag}(\sqrt{\lambda_1},\dots,\sqrt{\lambda_n})$; the real square roots exist
because every $\lambda_k > 0$. It squares correctly, again using $Q^\top Q = I$:
$$
A^{1/2}A^{1/2}
= Q\Lambda^{1/2}(Q^\top Q)\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top
= Q\Lambda^{1/2}\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top
= Q\Lambda Q^\top = A .
$$
It is symmetric ($(Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top)^\top = Q\Lambda^{1/2}Q^\top$ since $\Lambda^{1/2}$
is diagonal) and its eigenvalues are the $\sqrt{\lambda_k} > 0$, so by @thm-pd-eig it is
positive-definite. (Uniqueness among *PD symmetric* square roots is true and is taken up in
[GEOM-19]; here we only need existence.) $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The square root is the engine of **whitening**: if $\Sigma$ is a PD covariance, then
$\Sigma^{-1/2} = Q\Lambda^{-1/2}Q^\top$ transforms correlated data into uncorrelated,
unit-variance data — the move at the heart of generalized least squares ([REGR-11]) and of
the Mahalanobis distance. [GEOM-19] develops this square root alongside the *triangular*
Cholesky factor, which does a related job more cheaply; for now the point is simply that
positive-definiteness is the exact condition under which "take the square root of a matrix"
even makes sense (you cannot take a real square root of a negative eigenvalue).

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Positive-definite is not the same as "all entries positive," and the two have nothing to
do with each other.** The matrix $\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1
\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ has all-positive entries but eigenvalues $3$ and $-1$, so it is
*indefinite* — its form is a saddle. Conversely $\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2
\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ has a negative entry but eigenvalues $1$ and $3$, so it is PD. Sign
of *entries* and sign of *eigenvalues* are unrelated; definiteness is always about the
eigenvalues (equivalently, the quadratic form), never the entry signs. The only entrywise
fact that holds is the necessary screen from earlier: the *diagonal* entries of a PD matrix
must be positive.
:::

## Why $A^\top A$ is the canonical PSD matrix {#sec-gram}

We now prove the single fact that explains why positive-(semi)definiteness is *everywhere*
in data science rather than an occasional curiosity. The matrices that arise from data are
overwhelmingly of the form $A^\top A$ — the **Gram matrix** of $A$'s columns ([GEOM-10]) —
and these are *automatically* PSD, with definiteness governed by a clean rank condition.

::: {#thm-gram}
**Theorem (the Gram matrix is PSD, and PD iff full column rank).** Let $A$ be any real
$m\times n$ matrix (not necessarily square or symmetric). Then $A^\top A$ is symmetric and
positive-semidefinite. Moreover, $A^\top A$ is positive-*definite* if and only if the
columns of $A$ are linearly independent (equivalently, $A$ has full column rank $n$).
:::

::: {.proof}
**Symmetric.** $(A^\top A)^\top = A^\top (A^\top)^\top = A^\top A$ by the transpose rules
$(MN)^\top = N^\top M^\top$ and $(M^\top)^\top = M$ ([GEOM-10]). So $A^\top A$ equals its
own transpose.

**PSD — the key identity.** Take any $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and compute its quadratic form.
Group the product as $(A^\top A)$ acting between $x^\top$ and $x$, then re-associate:
$$
x^\top (A^\top A) x = (x^\top A^\top)(A x) = (Ax)^\top (Ax) = \|Ax\|^2 .
$$
The middle step used $(Ax)^\top = x^\top A^\top$ ([GEOM-10]); the last is the definition of
the squared norm, $v^\top v = \|v\|^2$ ([GEOM-04]). So the quadratic form of $A^\top A$ at
$x$ is **literally the squared length of the vector $Ax$** — and a squared length is never
negative:
$$
x^\top (A^\top A) x = \|Ax\|^2 \ge 0 \quad\text{for every } x .
$$ {#eq-gram-norm}
That is precisely PSD by @def-pd. This is the cleanest derivation in the article, and the
identity $x^\top A^\top A x = \|Ax\|^2$ is worth carrying: *the Gram matrix's quadratic form
measures how long $A$ makes the input.*

**PD $\iff$ full column rank.** From @eq-gram-norm, $A^\top A$ fails to be PD exactly when
the inequality is *not strict* for some nonzero $x$ — that is, when there is an $x \neq 0$
with
$$
\|Ax\|^2 = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Ax = 0
$$
(a vector has zero length iff it is the zero vector, [GEOM-04]). So:

- If the columns of $A$ are **independent**, the only solution of $Ax = 0$ is $x = 0$ (that
  is the definition of independence — no nontrivial combination of the columns vanishes,
  [GEOM-03]). Then for every $x \neq 0$ we have $Ax \neq 0$, hence $\|Ax\|^2 > 0$, hence
  $x^\top A^\top A x > 0$: **$A^\top A$ is PD.**
- If the columns are **dependent**, there is some $x \neq 0$ with $Ax = 0$, and then
  $x^\top A^\top A x = \|Ax\|^2 = 0$ with $x \neq 0$: the form hits zero off the origin, so
  **$A^\top A$ is PSD but not PD.**

The two phrasings in the theorem statement are the same condition: "$A$ has full column
rank $n$" is *defined* as "the $n$ columns of $A$ are linearly independent" ([GEOM-12]), so
the rank-$n$ case is exactly the independent-columns case just handled, and the
rank-deficient case is exactly the dependent-columns case. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This theorem is why the three motivating objects of @sec-why are PD or PSD without anyone
having to check eigenvalues by hand:

- **Covariance.** The sample covariance is $\Sigma = \tfrac{1}{n-1}X_c^\top X_c$ for the
  centered data matrix $X_c$, which is $\tfrac{1}{n-1}$ times a Gram matrix — automatically
  PSD by @thm-gram, and PD exactly when the centered features are linearly independent (no
  feature is an exact linear combination of the others). A degenerate, redundant feature set
  is *precisely* what makes a covariance only PSD, the boundary case we explore in
  @sec-ex-edge.
- **Normal equations.** $X^\top X$ is a Gram matrix, PSD always, and PD exactly when the
  design matrix $X$ has full column rank — which is the condition for a *unique* least-squares
  fit. When predictors are collinear, $X^\top X$ drops to merely PSD (singular), $\det = 0$,
  and there is no unique $\hat\beta$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is every positive-semidefinite matrix of the form $A^\top A$?"* Yes — and the square root
corollary basically proves it. If $M$ is symmetric PSD, then by the PSD analogue of
@cor-sqrt it has a symmetric PSD square root $M^{1/2}$ (real, since all $\lambda_k \ge 0$),
and $M = M^{1/2}M^{1/2} = (M^{1/2})^\top M^{1/2}$ — so $M = A^\top A$ with $A = M^{1/2}$.
The Gram form and the PSD property are therefore two descriptions of the same class of
matrices. That equivalence is the linear-algebra reason the SVD and PCA, which factor data
matrices through $A^\top A$, are so closely tied to the spectral theory of PSD matrices
([GEOM-20]).
:::

# Three tests for positive-definiteness {#sec-tests}

The eigenvalue characterization (@thm-pd-eig) is the *meaning* of positive-definiteness,
but in practice you reach for whichever test fits the situation. We give the three standard
ones, derive the part that needs deriving, and say plainly when to use each.

## Test 1 — the eigenvalues {#sec-eig-test}

**Compute the eigenvalues; $A$ is PD iff all are $> 0$, PSD iff all are $\ge 0$.** This is
@thm-pd-eig verbatim. It is the conceptually correct test and the one that *explains* the
property, and for small or moderate matrices it is what you would do. Its cost is that of a
full symmetric eigendecomposition (a `numpy.linalg.eigvalsh` call), which is more work than
strictly necessary if all you want is a yes/no answer. Numerically, beware that an
eigenvalue computed as a tiny negative number like $-10^{-15}$ on a matrix that is
mathematically PSD is almost always *round-off*, not a real negative direction — at the
exact PD/PSD boundary, finite-precision arithmetic cannot tell $0$ from a hair below it,
which is itself the practical lesson of @sec-ex-edge.

## Test 2 — Sylvester's criterion (leading principal minors) {#sec-sylvester}

For checking a small matrix *by hand* — especially a symbolic one — the determinant test is
often fastest because it needs no eigenvalues at all.

::: {#thm-sylvester}
**Theorem (Sylvester's criterion).** A symmetric matrix $A$ is positive-definite if and
only if all of its **leading principal minors** are positive. The $k$-th leading principal
minor $D_k$ is the determinant of the top-left $k\times k$ block of $A$:
$$
D_1 = A_{11}, \quad
D_2 = \det\!\begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{21} & A_{22}\end{bmatrix}, \quad \dots,
\quad D_n = \det A .
$$
$A$ is PD $\iff D_1 > 0,\ D_2 > 0,\ \dots,\ D_n > 0$.
:::

We do not re-derive the general criterion from scratch (its cleanest proof rides on the
Cholesky construction of [GEOM-19], and we flag that rather than bury a long induction
here). But the $2\times 2$ case is short, illuminating, and the one you will actually use by
hand, so we prove *it* completely — and it already shows why the test works.

::: {#lem-sylvester2}
**Lemma (Sylvester for $2\times 2$).** A symmetric $A = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} a & b \\
b & c \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ is positive-definite if and only if $a > 0$ and $ac - b^2 >
0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write out the quadratic form for $x = (x_1, x_2)^\top$ and **complete the square** — the
same Calc-I-level algebra used to graph a parabola:
$$
x^\top A x = a x_1^2 + 2b x_1 x_2 + c x_2^2 .
$$
(The cross-term is $2b x_1 x_2$ because the symmetric off-diagonal contributes $b$ twice,
from $A_{12}$ and $A_{21}$.) Assume first $a > 0$, so we may factor it out of the $x_1$
terms and complete the square in $x_1$:
$$
x^\top A x
= a\Bigl(x_1^2 + \tfrac{2b}{a}x_1 x_2\Bigr) + c x_2^2
= a\Bigl(x_1 + \tfrac{b}{a}x_2\Bigr)^2 - a\cdot\tfrac{b^2}{a^2}x_2^2 + c x_2^2 ,
$$
where the subtracted term $-a(b/a)^2 x_2^2 = -(b^2/a)x_2^2$ undoes the cross-term we added
inside the square. Collecting the $x_2^2$ terms over the common denominator $a$:
$$
x^\top A x = a\Bigl(x_1 + \tfrac{b}{a}x_2\Bigr)^2 + \frac{ac - b^2}{a}\,x_2^2 .
$$ {#eq-completed-square}
Now read off both directions.

**($\Leftarrow$)** If $a > 0$ and $ac - b^2 > 0$, then both coefficients in
@eq-completed-square are positive ($a > 0$ and $(ac-b^2)/a > 0$), so $x^\top A x$ is a sum
of two nonnegative squares. It is zero only if *both* squares vanish: $x_2 = 0$ (from the
second term) and then $x_1 + (b/a)\cdot 0 = x_1 = 0$ (from the first). So $x^\top A x = 0$
forces $x = 0$, i.e. $x^\top A x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$: $A$ is PD.

**($\Rightarrow$)** Suppose $A$ is PD. Testing $x = e_1 = (1,0)^\top$ gives $a = e_1^\top A
e_1 > 0$, our first condition. Now choose the direction that isolates the second term: take
$x_2 = 1$ and $x_1 = -b/a$ (which kills the first square). Then @eq-completed-square gives
$$
x^\top A x = a\cdot 0 + \frac{ac-b^2}{a}\cdot 1 = \frac{ac-b^2}{a} ,
$$
and PD forces this to be $> 0$; since $a > 0$, that means $ac - b^2 > 0$, the second
condition. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Notice the proof *is* the geometry: completing the square rewrites the form as a sum of
squares with the leading minors as coefficients, so "all minors positive" is just "all the
squared-coefficient stretches are positive." Sylvester's test is fast by hand and needs no
eigenvalue solving, but it has two cautions: it is a test for **strict** PD only (the minor
version for PSD requires checking *all* principal minors, not just the leading ones, and is
fiddly), and computing $n$ growing determinants is numerically poor for large matrices.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Can I check PSD by relaxing Sylvester to 'all leading principal minors $\ge 0$'?"* **No —
this is a classic trap.** The matrix $A = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1
\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ has leading minors $D_1 = 0 \ge 0$ and $D_2 = 0 \ge 0$, yet it is
*not* PSD: $x = (0,1)^\top$ gives $x^\top A x = -1 < 0$. For PSD you must check **every
principal minor** (all $\binom{n}{k}$ of them, from every subset of rows/columns, not just
the top-left ones), or — far better — just check the eigenvalues are $\ge 0$. The
leading-minor shortcut is valid only for *strict* PD. Interviewers love this one because
the natural-looking generalization is wrong.
:::

## Test 3 — "a Cholesky factor exists" {#sec-cholesky-test}

The test used inside numerical libraries is the most efficient of all: **try to factor $A =
L L^\top$ with $L$ lower-triangular and positive diagonal (the Cholesky factorization); the
factorization runs to completion if and only if $A$ is positive-definite.** The construction
proceeds entry by entry, and at each step it must take the square root of a quantity that is
positive exactly when the matrix is PD — so a non-PD matrix is caught the instant that
quantity goes nonpositive, and the algorithm reports failure. We only *flag* this test here;
the full constructive derivation of $L$, and the proof that its existence is equivalent to
positive-definiteness, is the subject of [GEOM-19]. The reason it is the practitioner's
default is cost and robustness: Cholesky is about twice as fast as a general factorization,
and "did the factorization succeed?" is a clean, stable yes/no that does not require
interpreting a near-zero eigenvalue.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In code, how would you actually test positive-definiteness — and why not just look at the
determinant?"* Attempt a Cholesky factorization (`numpy.linalg.cholesky`) and catch the
failure: success means PD, a `LinAlgError` means not PD. **Do not** test PD by checking
$\det A > 0$ — the determinant is the *product* of eigenvalues, so it is positive whenever
an *even* number of eigenvalues are negative. The indefinite matrix
$\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ has $\det = +1 > 0$ but
is negative-definite — a positive determinant is fully consistent with the matrix being a
saddle or an upside-down bowl. The determinant tells you about invertibility, not
definiteness.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the tests on real, messy matrices — never one rigged to flatter the property. The
first is done entirely by hand so you watch all three tests agree; the second is the
covariance matrix of a genuine dataset; and the third sits right at the PSD boundary, the
honest edge where the property fails and tells us something true about the data.

## Example 1 — one matrix, all three tests, by hand {#sec-ex-byhand}

Take the symmetric matrix
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad A = A^\top .
$$
We verify it is PD three independent ways.

**Test 2 (Sylvester), fastest by hand.** The leading minors are
$$
D_1 = A_{11} = 2 > 0, \qquad
D_2 = \det A = (2)(2) - (-1)(-1) = 4 - 1 = 3 > 0 .
$$
Both positive, so by @thm-sylvester (or @lem-sylvester2 with $a=2,\ c=2,\ b=-1$:
$a>0$ and $ac-b^2 = 3 > 0$) $A$ is **positive-definite**.

**Test 1 (eigenvalues).** The characteristic polynomial ([GEOM-14]) is
$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = (2-\lambda)^2 - (-1)(-1) = (2-\lambda)^2 - 1 ,
$$
so $(2-\lambda)^2 = 1$, $2 - \lambda = \pm 1$, giving $\lambda_1 = 3$ and $\lambda_2 = 1$.
Both positive — PD confirmed, and consistent with $D_2 = \det A = \lambda_1\lambda_2 = 3$
and $\operatorname{tr}A = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 4$.

**Test 3 (Cholesky), by hand.** Seek lower-triangular $L = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} \ell_{11}
& 0 \\ \ell_{21} & \ell_{22}\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ with $LL^\top = A$. Multiplying out,
$$
LL^\top = \begin{bmatrix} \ell_{11}^2 & \ell_{11}\ell_{21} \\
\ell_{11}\ell_{21} & \ell_{21}^2 + \ell_{22}^2 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} .
$$
Match entries top-left to bottom-right: $\ell_{11}^2 = 2 \Rightarrow \ell_{11} = \sqrt 2$
(real, positive — the first place PD is needed); then $\ell_{11}\ell_{21} = -1 \Rightarrow
\ell_{21} = -1/\sqrt 2$; finally $\ell_{21}^2 + \ell_{22}^2 = 2 \Rightarrow \ell_{22}^2 = 2
- \tfrac12 = \tfrac32 \Rightarrow \ell_{22} = \sqrt{3/2}$ (real, positive again). The
factorization completed with real positive diagonal, so $A$ is PD. Had any of those
under-the-root quantities been negative, the algorithm would have stopped — that is exactly
how Cholesky detects non-PD matrices.

All three tests agree. @fig-ex-byhand draws the bowl and confirms numerically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-byhand
#| fig-cap: 'The hand-worked PD matrix $A = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}2&-1\\-1&2\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$. **Left:** the quadratic-form surface $q(x)=x^\top A x$ is a bowl opening upward in every direction, with its single lowest point at the origin. **Right:** the level sets $q(x)=c$ are concentric ellipses whose axes lie along the eigenvectors $q_1$ (red, eigenvalue $\lambda_1=3$, the *steeper* direction, so the shorter ellipse axis) and $q_2$ (green, $\lambda_2=1$, the gentler direction, longer axis). All eigenvalues positive $\Leftrightarrow$ bowl $\Leftrightarrow$ positive-definite.'
A = np.array([[2.0, -1.0], [-1.0, 2.0]])
w, V = np.linalg.eigh(A)                  # ascending
order = np.argsort(w)[::-1]; w, V = w[order], V[:, order]
q1, q2 = V[:, 0], V[:, 1]

g = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 80)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
Z = A[0, 0]*GX**2 + 2*A[0, 1]*GX*GY + A[1, 1]*GY**2

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10.6, 4.4))
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
axL.plot_surface(GX, GY, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.92, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
axL.scatter([0], [0], [0], color=HILITE, s=45, depthshade=False)
axL.set_title(r"bowl: $q(x)=x^\top A x$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axL.set_zlabel("$q$")
axL.view_init(elev=24, azim=-58)

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
axR.set_aspect("equal")
cs = axR.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=[0.5, 1.5, 3, 5, 8], colors=VEC, linewidths=1.3)
axR.clabel(cs, inline=True, fontsize=8)
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(q1), color=HILITE, label=r"$q_1\ (\lambda=3)$", label_offset=(0.0, 0.35))
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(q2), color=ACCENT, label=r"$q_2\ (\lambda=1)$", label_offset=(0.5, 0.1))
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4); axR.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.6, alpha=0.4)
axR.set_xlim(-2.4, 2.4); axR.set_ylim(-2.4, 2.4)
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axR.set_title("level sets are ellipses along eigenvectors")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("eigenvalues:", np.round(np.sort(np.linalg.eigvalsh(A))[::-1], 6), " (all > 0  => PD)")
print("leading minors: D1 =", A[0, 0], " D2 = det A =", round(np.linalg.det(A), 6))
L = np.linalg.cholesky(A)
print("Cholesky L =\n", np.round(L, 4), "\n L L^T == A:", np.allclose(L @ L.T, A))
```

## Example 2 — a covariance matrix is positive-definite {#sec-ex-covariance}

The PD matrix a data scientist meets most often is a **covariance matrix** of full-rank
data. We build one from three genuinely correlated features — height, weight, and a derived
"build" score — and confirm, via @thm-gram, that it is PD because the features are
independent (no one is an exact linear combination of the others). This is the matrix you
would invert to form a Gaussian density or a Mahalanobis distance, so its
positive-definiteness is exactly what makes those constructions legal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-covariance
#| fig-cap: 'A full-rank covariance matrix is positive-definite. **Left:** the $3\times3$ sample covariance $\Sigma = \tfrac{1}{n-1}X_c^\top X_c$ of three correlated features, a Gram matrix and so PSD by construction; because the features are linearly independent it is strictly PD. **Right:** its three eigenvalues, all positive — the variances along the three perpendicular principal axes of the data cloud. All bars above the zero line is the visual signature of positive-definiteness.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 400
height = rng.normal(170, 9, n)                      # cm
weight = 0.9 * height - 90 + rng.normal(0, 6, n)    # correlated with height
build  = 0.5 * weight - 0.2 * height + rng.normal(0, 4, n)  # correlated, but independent column
X = np.column_stack([height, weight, build])
Xc = X - X.mean(axis=0)
Sigma = (Xc.T @ Xc) / (n - 1)
evals = np.sort(np.linalg.eigvalsh(Sigma))[::-1]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 3.8))
im = axL.imshow(Sigma, cmap="viridis")
axL.set_xticks(range(3)); axL.set_yticks(range(3))
axL.set_xticklabels(["height", "weight", "build"], fontsize=8, rotation=30)
axL.set_yticklabels(["height", "weight", "build"], fontsize=8)
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(3):
        axL.text(j, i, f"{Sigma[i, j]:.0f}", ha="center", va="center",
                 color="white", fontsize=9)
axL.set_title(r"covariance $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}X_c^\top X_c$")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axL, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)

axR.bar(range(3), evals, color=[HILITE, ACCENT, VEC])
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axR.set_xticks(range(3)); axR.set_xticklabels([r"$\lambda_1$", r"$\lambda_2$", r"$\lambda_3$"])
axR.set_ylabel("eigenvalue (variance along axis)")
axR.set_title("all eigenvalues > 0  =>  PD")
plt.tight_layout()

print("eigenvalues:", np.round(evals, 3), " (all > 0 => positive-definite)")
try:
    np.linalg.cholesky(Sigma)
    print("Cholesky succeeded => positive-definite (Test 3 agrees)")
except np.linalg.LinAlgError:
    print("Cholesky failed => not PD")
```

The covariance has all-positive eigenvalues and a successful Cholesky — PD by every test.
Geometrically (the reading from [GEOM-16]) the eigenvalues are the variances along the
cloud's perpendicular principal axes; positive-definiteness says the cloud has genuine
spread in *every* direction, no flattened-to-a-pancake axis. That last phrase is the hinge
to the edge case: what happens when the cloud *does* flatten?

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: a singular covariance (PSD, not PD) {#sec-ex-edge}

Here is the honest boundary the constitution demands, and it is not contrived — it is the
single most common way positive-definiteness fails in practice. Suppose one feature is an
exact linear combination of the others: a "total" column that is the sum of two component
columns, or a unit conversion duplicated (weight in kilograms *and* in pounds), or — the
canonical regression sin — a set of one-hot dummy variables that sum to the all-ones column.
Then the data cloud is genuinely *flat* in one direction, the columns are linearly
dependent, and by @thm-gram the covariance (and the normal-equations matrix $X^\top X$) is
PSD **but not PD**: it has a zero eigenvalue, is singular, and cannot be inverted. The
Gaussian density and the unique least-squares fit both cease to exist.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: a redundant feature kills positive-definiteness. We take two real features and append a third, $f_3 = f_1 + f_2$ (an exact 'total' column). The covariance is now PSD but **not** PD. **Left:** its eigenvalues — two positive, and one that is *zero* to machine precision (the flat direction $f_1 + f_2 - f_3 = 0$). **Right:** the data cloud projected onto the plane of $f_1, f_2$ and the perpendicular contribution of $f_3$: the points lie exactly on a tilted plane (zero thickness in one direction), the geometric meaning of the zero eigenvalue. Cholesky fails; the matrix has no inverse."
rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 300
f1 = rng.normal(0, 2, n)
f2 = 0.6 * f1 + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)
f3 = f1 + f2                                  # exact linear dependence
X = np.column_stack([f1, f2, f3])
Xc = X - X.mean(axis=0)
Sigma = (Xc.T @ Xc) / (n - 1)
evals = np.sort(np.linalg.eigvalsh(Sigma))[::-1]

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(10.2, 4.0))
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1)
axL.bar(range(3), evals, color=[HILITE, ACCENT, GREY])
axL.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axL.set_xticks(range(3)); axL.set_xticklabels([r"$\lambda_1$", r"$\lambda_2$", r"$\lambda_3$"])
axL.set_ylabel("eigenvalue")
axL.set_title("one eigenvalue is zero  =>  PSD, not PD")
axL.annotate("flat direction\n$\\lambda_3\\approx 0$", xy=(2, 0), xytext=(1.2, evals[0]*0.4),
             fontsize=9, color=GREY, arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY))

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2, projection="3d")
axR.scatter(Xc[:, 0], Xc[:, 1], Xc[:, 2], s=10, color=PALE, alpha=0.7)
axR.set_xlabel("$f_1$", fontsize=9); axR.set_ylabel("$f_2$", fontsize=9)
axR.set_zlabel("$f_3$", fontsize=9)
axR.set_title("cloud lies on a plane (zero thickness)")
axR.view_init(elev=18, azim=-70)
plt.tight_layout()

print("eigenvalues:", np.round(evals, 8), "  (smallest ~ 0 => singular, PSD not PD)")
print("det Sigma =", f"{np.linalg.det(Sigma):.3e}", " (~0 => not invertible)")
try:
    np.linalg.cholesky(Sigma)
    print("Cholesky succeeded")
except np.linalg.LinAlgError as e:
    print("Cholesky FAILED:", e, " => matrix is not positive-definite")
```

This is the failure that matters most, because it is *informative*, not a numerical
accident. A zero eigenvalue in a covariance or $X^\top X$ is the matrix telling you that
your features carry redundant information — the cloud has no spread along $f_1 + f_2 - f_3$,
because that combination is identically zero. The fixes are modeling fixes, and which one
you reach for is real practitioner judgment:

- **Drop the redundant feature** (the cleanest fix when the dependence is exact and known —
  e.g. remove one dummy from a complete one-hot set, the "drop a level" rule of regression).
- **Regularize**: add a small positive multiple of the identity, $X^\top X + \lambda I$,
  which shifts every eigenvalue up by $\lambda$ and so *guarantees* positive-definiteness
  (every $\lambda_k + \lambda > 0$). This is **ridge regression** ([REGR-14]) seen from the
  linear-algebra side, and it is exactly why ridge always has a unique solution even when
  OLS does not.
- **Use a pseudoinverse** ([GEOM-21]), which gives the minimum-norm solution on the
  non-flat subspace and simply ignores the flat direction.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**"Nearly singular" is more dangerous than "singular," and definiteness will not warn you.**
The exact dependence above produces a clean zero eigenvalue you can catch. The realistic
case is a *tiny* positive eigenvalue — features that are highly but not perfectly collinear.
The matrix is then *technically* positive-definite (Cholesky succeeds, all eigenvalues
$>0$), so every PD test says "fine," yet it is so close to the boundary that inverting it
amplifies noise enormously: the bowl is a near-flat trough, and its bottom slides wildly
under tiny data changes. Positive-definiteness is a *binary* yes/no; it cannot see *how
close* to the cliff you are. The quantity that measures that distance is the **condition
number** $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ ([GEOM-26]) — a PD matrix with
$\kappa = 10^{12}$ passes every test in this article and is still numerically useless. Pass
the definiteness test *and* check the condition number.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest scope {#sec-failure}

Positive-definiteness is a clean property, but knowing *when its presence or absence is
telling you something real* is the practitioner skill. We collect the boundaries.

**When the property genuinely holds, lean on it fully.** A PD matrix gives you everything at
once: a unique minimizer of its quadratic form, an invertible (and PD) inverse, a real
square root, a Cholesky factor for fast and stable solves, and a guarantee that any
optimization built on it (as a Hessian) is locally convex with a true minimum. This is why
so much of applied mathematics works hard to *arrange* a PD matrix — centering data so the
covariance is well-conditioned, adding ridge so $X^\top X + \lambda I$ is PD, choosing
parameterizations so the Hessian is PD near the optimum.

**When it is only PSD (a zero eigenvalue), the failure is structural, not numerical.** As in
@sec-ex-edge, a genuine zero eigenvalue means an exact redundancy or a flat direction in the
data. The right response is to fix the model (drop, regularize, or pseudoinvert), not to
nudge the matrix and pretend. A PSD-not-PD covariance is a *valid* object (it still
describes a degenerate-but-real distribution supported on a lower-dimensional subspace) —
what fails is *inversion*, so any method that needs $\Sigma^{-1}$ must be replaced by one
that works on the subspace.

**When it is indefinite, your modeling assumption is probably wrong.** A symmetric matrix
that was *supposed* to be PD but comes out indefinite (some negative eigenvalue) is a red
flag. A covariance estimated by a shortcut (pairwise-complete correlations, or a shrinkage
gone wrong) can come out indefinite — which is nonsense for a covariance and signals the
estimator is broken; the fix is a proper PSD estimator or nearest-PSD projection. A Hessian
that is indefinite at a point you hoped was a minimum is telling you the truth: that point
is a *saddle*, and the optimizer must keep moving. Do not "repair" an indefinite matrix by
flipping signs of eigenvalues unless you understand what the negative directions mean — they
are usually information, not error.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is positive-definiteness the right thing to require, and when are you forcing it
where it doesn't belong?"* The summary you should be able to recite:

- **Require it** for covariance matrices you intend to invert (Gaussian density, Mahalanobis
  distance, GLS whitening), for $X^\top X$ when you want a unique least-squares fit, and for
  a Hessian when you are claiming a local minimum. In these, PD is not a convenience — it is
  the existence condition for the answer.
- **Settle for PSD** when the object is genuinely degenerate and you only need the quadratic
  form's value or a projection, not an inverse (rank-deficient designs handled by a
  pseudoinverse, PCA on data with redundant features, covariance of a deterministic linear
  combination).
- **Do not force it** when the matrix is honestly indefinite and the negative directions
  carry meaning — a saddle in a loss surface, a curvature that genuinely bends down. Forcing
  PD there (e.g. clipping negative eigenvalues to zero) discards real structure. The honest
  move is to understand the negative directions, not to legislate them away.
:::

# How you would actually compute this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. To *test* positive-definiteness
in code, the idiom is to attempt a Cholesky factorization and catch the exception:
`numpy.linalg.cholesky(A)` succeeds iff $A$ is PD (to numerical tolerance), and is the
fastest reliable test. To *use* a PD matrix, never form $A^{-1}$ explicitly when you only
need to solve $A x = b$ — factor once with Cholesky and solve the two triangular systems
(`scipy.linalg.cho_factor` / `cho_solve`), which is faster and far more numerically stable
than inversion ([GEOM-26]'s "solve, don't invert"). To *inspect* the property and its
margin, `numpy.linalg.eigvalsh(A)` returns the (sorted, real) eigenvalues; check the
smallest is positive *and* compute $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ to gauge how
close to the boundary you are. Every one of these routines is doing exactly the algebra of
this article — the eigenvalues of @thm-pd-eig, the Cholesky existence of @sec-cholesky-test
— and you now know what each is checking and why.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-16 *Symmetric Matrices and the Spectral Theorem*]** (which gave us
$A = Q\Lambda Q^\top$) and **[GEOM-17 *Quadratic Forms*]** (which gave us $x^\top A x =
\sum_k \lambda_k z_k^2$). This article asked the one question those left open — *are the
stretches positive?* — and named the answer positive-definiteness, the precise algebraic
form of "the surface is a bowl." From here:

- **[GEOM-19 *Cholesky and the Matrix Square Root*]** — the factorizations a PD matrix
  *admits*: $A = LL^\top$ (triangular, the basis of Test 3) and $A^{1/2} = Q\Lambda^{1/2}
  Q^\top$ (symmetric, the whitening tool of @cor-sqrt). Their existence is equivalent to the
  positive-definiteness proved here.
- **[GEOM-24 *The Hessian and the Curvature of a Surface*]** and **[GEOM-25 *The Gradient
  and Hessian of a Quadratic Form*]** — where definiteness becomes the second-derivative
  test: a PD Hessian means a genuine local minimum, the exact statement that the loss surface
  is locally a bowl.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — the refinement the Pitfall in
  @sec-ex-edge demanded: definiteness is binary, but $\kappa = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$
  measures *how* definite, and that number predicts whether a PD problem is actually solvable
  in floating point.

Downstream of the track, this is the article behind a **valid Gaussian** (Probability /
Inference need a PD covariance), behind **GLS and whitening** (Regression [REGR-11], via the
square root), behind **ridge regression** ([REGR-14], which adds $\lambda I$ precisely to
restore positive-definiteness), and behind every **convexity test** in Optimization.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Where the idea was forged.** The theory of quadratic forms and their signs grew out of
  Gauss's *Disquisitiones Arithmeticae* (1801) and Lagrange's work on minima of quadratic
  functions; the determinant test is **J. J. Sylvester**'s "law of inertia" and criterion
  from the 1850s, which also gave us the word *definite* for a form of fixed sign. Any
  graduate linear-algebra text (Strang's *Linear Algebra and Its Applications*; Horn &
  Johnson's *Matrix Analysis*, ch. 7, the definitive modern reference on PD matrices)
  develops all three tests and the full PSD principal-minor story.
- **The threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why does the Cholesky factor exist
  exactly when $A$ is PD, and how is it built?* — that is the constructive heart of
  [GEOM-19], and it also supplies the clean proof of the general Sylvester criterion we only
  stated. *How close to the boundary is "too close"?* — the condition number, [GEOM-26].
  *What replaces inversion when the matrix is only PSD?* — the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse,
  [GEOM-21]. *And the statistical meaning of a covariance being PD* — that a Gaussian exists
  and is non-degenerate — is owned by the Probability and Inference tracks, which import the
  algebra proved here.
